```{r, echo=FALSE, results="hide"}
set.seed(sample.int(.Machine$integer.max, 1))

## Parametros
alpha <- 0.05
mu0   <- 5
sigma <- 2
n     <- sample(c(25, 36, 49, 64), 1)
mu1   <- mu0 + 2

## Poder do teste NP
Poder <- round(1 - pnorm(qnorm(1 - alpha) - (mu1 - mu0) / (sigma / sqrt(n))), 4)

## Respostas
correct <- Poder
w1 <- round(Poder - 0.1, 4)
w2 <- round(1 - Poder, 4)
w3 <- round(Poder + 0.1, 4)
w4 <- round(0.05, 4)

all_vals <- c(correct, w1, w2, w3, w4)
# Garante cinco alternativas numericamente distintas após a parametrização.
integer_choices <- all(abs(all_vals - round(all_vals)) < sqrt(.Machine$double.eps))
choice_step <- if (integer_choices) 1 else max(1e-4, max(abs(all_vals), na.rm = TRUE) * 0.01)
while (anyDuplicated(all_vals)) {
  duplicate_position <- which(duplicated(all_vals))[1]
  all_vals[duplicate_position] <- max(all_vals, na.rm = TRUE) + choice_step
}
choices <- sample(all_vals)
sol_pos <- which(choices == correct)
sol_str <- paste(as.integer(seq_along(choices) == sol_pos), collapse = "")
```

Question
========
Para $H_0:\mu = `r mu0`$ vs. $H_1:\mu = `r mu1`$ com $\sigma = `r sigma`$, $n = `r n`$ e $\alpha = 0{,}05$, o teste de Neyman-Pearson tem poder:

Answerlist
----------
* `r choices[1]`
* `r choices[2]`
* `r choices[3]`
* `r choices[4]`
* `r choices[5]`

Solution
========
Poder $= 1 - \Phi\!\left(z_{0{,}95} - \frac{\mu_1-\mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(1{,}645 - \frac{`r mu1-mu0`}{`r sigma`/\sqrt{`r n`}}\right) = `r Poder`$.

Meta-information
================
exname: Cap6S2Q8_NP_tamanho_poder
extype: schoice
exsolution: `r sol_str`
exshuffle: FALSE
